questions.questionText: Which of the following bacterial cell structures is primarily responsible for preventing osmotic lysis?
questions.explanation: The rigid peptidoglycan layer protects bacteria from osmotic pressure.

questions.questionText: Gram-positive bacteria differ from Gram-negative bacteria in that they have:
questions.optionA: A thicker peptidoglycan layer and teichoic acids
questions.explanation: Gram-positive cell walls are thick peptidoglycan with embedded teichoic acids.

questions.questionText: Endospores in bacteria are highly resistant primarily due to the presence of:
questions.explanation: Dipicolinic acid complexed with calcium dehydrates and protects the spore.

questions.questionText: Which bacterial appendage allows for the transfer of DNA between cells (conjugation)?
questions.explanation: The sex pilus connects a donor and recipient cell for plasmid transfer.

questions.questionText: In bacterial growth curves, secondary metabolites like antibiotics are typically produced during the:
questions.explanation: Production of secondary metabolites occurs when nutrients deplete in stationary phase.
Plasmids replicate independently and often carry accessory genes (e.g., resistance).

questions.questionText: An organism that requires oxygen to grow but is killed by atmospheric levels (~21% O2) is called a:
questions.explanation: Microaerophiles need O2 at lower concentrations (e.g., Campylobacter, Helicobacter).

questions.questionText: The transfer of bacterial DNA from one cell to another via a bacteriophage is termed:
questions.explanation: Transduction involves a viral vector (phage) moving host bacterial DNA.

questions.questionText: Which of the following sterilization methods reliably destroys bacterial endospores?
questions.explanation: Moist heat under pressure is required to kill highly resistant spores.

questions.questionText: Endotoxin (Lipopolysaccharide, LPS) is an integral component of the cell wall of:
questions.explanation: LPS is located in the outer leaflet of the Gram-negative outer membrane.

questions.questionText: In General Microbiology, which statement is most accurate regarding the core definition and diagnostic concept?
questions.optionA: It reflects the most exam-relevant definition used in standard Microbiology texts
questions.optionB: It is an obsolete definition no longer used in clinical practice
questions.optionC: It is used only in pediatric population and never in adults
questions.optionD: It has no diagnostic significance in NEET-PG pattern questions
questions.explanation: This tests foundational definition-level knowledge from standard Microbiology references for General Microbiology. Subject: Microbiology. Chapter: General Microbiology.

questions.questionText: The most important etiology/risk factor to suspect first in a typical General Microbiology scenario is:
questions.optionA: The common high-yield risk factor repeatedly asked in entrance exams
questions.optionB: A rare cause seen only in isolated case reports
questions.optionD: An etiology excluded by routine first-line workup
questions.explanation: NEET-PG and INI-CET focus on common, high-yield causes before rare differentials. Subject: Microbiology. Chapter: General Microbiology.
